sentences.text: That's right, to hide.
Because she wouldn't have-- she wouldn't have gone by herself.
So he-- he played for time till he saw her going up the stairs to the attics, which were more than one.
And she was really going there and the Germans-- my father had to open the gate downstairs-- they came in.
The neighbors.
Look, when they-- when they collect the Jews, it was a spectacle.
So the neighbors in the opposite side.
There were a lot of people who, who liked to watch it and liked to do it.
They were collaborators and they were also friends of Jews, but only on a very small scale and secretly.
Because you were not allowed to help the Jews.
Whoever was found hiding with a Jewish family, many of them were taken to jail.
And in the country, I heard they were even shot on the spot.
So to hide a Jew was a heroic thing.
And not many people wanted to do it.
Anyhow, she, she was not found.
They went from top to cellars.
We had huge cellars.
They, luckily, didn't find her.
But they took my father.
And they were asking, where are the children?
He said, I don't know.
They are not here.
For weeks, they are not living here.
So they, they searched the place, couldn't find anyone, and left.
My mother was hiding on the roof.
She was not in the attic.
Because of the fear, you are able to perform inhuman things.
She went with a little staircase which, which-- we are leaning on a pitched roof, literally.
And you know in Europe, you have got huge chimneys because in every room, you have a fireplace.
Each fireplace has a huge chimney sticking up the roof.
And there are little passageways because we have a chimney sweeper.
Once in three months maybe, the chimney sweeper comes and has to clean the chimneys.
Otherwise, you wouldn't have a proficient fireplace.
And my mother was daring.
She went on these boards and was walking and was hiding behind one of the big chimneys.
She was there the end of the night plus the next day.
She didn't dare to come down.
When the-- we called the woman who was our trustee [NON-ENGLISH], the proper-- that was also a Slovakian word.
She was the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means she-- the Aryan member of the public.
Therefore, it is [NON-ENGLISH] means Aryan.
And when the [NON-ENGLISH], when the trustee woman which knew us, when she came opened the shop the next day, she knew from the neighbors, oh, they took the Jews away.
And she was going up in the flat.
She had the keys.
And she couldn't find anybody.
She thought, God forbid, they took them all.
She didn't know.
But she heard my father was taken and beaten.
That's how we knew it.
But the mother was not there.
And she was not taken away.
She knew it.
She went several times to look for her.
She couldn't find her.
Only in the evening, before she-- because she was not living in the house, the trustee woman.
In the evening, she was so worried, she still went up and then she heard a, like a, like a small voice.
Yes, I am here.
I am here.
And that was my mother.
Poor thing.
She was there and she was waiting, saying somebody will discover.
She wouldn't have mind to be taken away.
And my, my sister-in-law, the trustee woman, she found her.
And she had a big problem.
How to save this poor woman?
What shall we do with her?
So she was able to, to hide her with one of the employees from the shop, which she was very good.
A poor woman.
Maybe she gave her money.
And she was hiding there for one night.
Then she had to find the other place.
And she was carrying her how?
In the evening from one place to the other till it was possible to, to save her life.
When it was very, very hard, because it became harder and harder, she arranged in her own initiative that my mother was taken to the country.
And the best part of it is, with the help of the other trustee.
The women who was complete strangers to us.
But these two women, while they were in the shop, and they were-- that was already more than two years, two and a half years almost That was in '42, beginning of '43, actually.
This woman became more and more friendly.
And we didn't know that, only after the war we found out, that Mimi-- that is the trustee woman of ours-- she said she couldn't bear it on her own.
So she disclosed this one day, especially when she found my mother.
She said, what will I do?
And she asked the other woman.
She said, look.
You are from the country because they were country people.
What can we do?
And she arranged, with the other woman, that my mother was secretly taken to the country town and was like a housekeeper with the parents of this second trustee woman which we had.
So it was a very nice gesture.
But don't forget, that was already in '44.
That was beginning of '44.
I was-- when, when I witnessed every night, or every week, or every month, the Jews were collected.
I was the only one.
I was the youngest and maybe more adventurous.
I said, we can't stay together.
That is silly because when they will come, we will be all together.
They will take us in one group.
So I, I got my first non-Jewish papers, false papers.
How did I get it?
I persuaded-- I was in the same age group, like the young-- how you call the volunteer in the shop?
Like the young, the young, the youngest member of our staff.
How you call this?
Like a clerk or an apprentice?
No, not a clerk.
No, apprentice.
Apprentice, right?
And so I got very friendly with one of the apprentices.
We had girls apprentices and, and boys.
And he was willing to give me his-- because everyone in a European country during the war had identity papers with photographs, all the particulars, like a passport.
And he gave me his identity papers.
You know, very courageous.
Because he knew our family and we were-- I can say that we were very good to our employees in the past and all the time.
So he, he was only obliged to help me.
He, I don't think how he made the decisions.
And he gave me his personal papers.
He knew that I will use it.
And they were, they were converted with my photograph and my details.
There were people who helped us to do it.
Anyhow, that was my first name, [NON-ENGLISH].
Because his name was [NON-ENGLISH].
I always tried to live under a presumed name starting with H.
Because my memory, already at that time, was bad.
So I thought if I have a name, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], they are all starting with H.
And when you are in a big problem, when the Gestapo takes you, then you forget your own birthday.
So the, the ideology of, of the same letter was a slight help.
So I packed a, a very shabby paper suitcase when, when we were still at home.
That happened before the, before the deportation of the majority of the Jews.
In '42, beginning of '42, I decided I will  live on my own.
But not in the country.
I was maybe naive.
I went to the outskirts of the town, like if you would go to Blacktown.
That was, for me, at that time, even further.
And I, I leased, I hired a room in a working class family that was a widow who made a living from leasing a room.
And I shared a room with a, a non-Jewish young man who was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I was maybe 20, 21.
And I was living there.
I, I packed a suitcase with shabby clothing, old one.
And even the dress was according to my papers, you know?
Labor class family.
And I was pretending that I am a uni student, beginner.
My parents were already in the part which was occupied, you know?
Like occupied, because the, the war was coming closer.
It was '43 and the Russians came closer.
So I always made, or arranged, for false papers to be in such area which is not traceable.
That was the advantage.
So I had my papers, the very first, the [NON-ENGLISH].
Was living in the Eastern part of the country where already one part was occupied by the Russian army.
So they couldn't trace it.
And that was very important.
Because otherwise, if I was living in this place and I, I-- during the day, the biggest problem was, what to, what to do the whole day.
So I was walking the streets.
And I had the little suitcase, briefcase, where I had some books.
And I pretended, when I came home at 5 or 6 o'clock in the evening, I pretended I came from the uni or studying.
And I had to do something.
So I had to create the image that I am a genuine student.
Because people, you have no idea, they watch you.
And even the woman who, who leased the room, if she would have known she's hiding a Jew-- mind you, it wasn't hiding.
It was living.
And that fellow who was sharing the room with me-- he was sleeping in one bed, I was in other.
[NON-ENGLISH] was a very nice, but simple, primitive fellow.
He went at 6 o'clock every morning to, to work.
He was working.
Because through our town, Danube, one of the biggest European rivers.
And we had a port which was connected right to the Black Sea.
So he went there as a wharf laborer.
And that went on for a couple of months, maybe three months.
I was quite-- as more you live this pretended life, as more you get used to it and your courage is boosted.
So I was there living quite happily.
I didn't have any contact anymore with the home.
And I had only a secret code with the trustee, Mimi, who's my future sister-in-law.
It was in case-- because when you live a life  like that, you can't have pocket full of money.
If they caught me and they would have seen that I have 1,000 or 2,000 crowns, which was a lot of money at that time, they would have said, oh.
This is not genuine.
How it can be?
So I had, maybe, 100 crown, which was peanuts.
And I arranged with her that if I'm in, in badly need, I will let her know, maybe phone in the night.
And she would arrange to send me some more money.
It happened very-- very, very seldom because it was dangerous.
And I, I didn't need much money because what I had, I was living very, very spartan life.
One day that I have to mention to you because that was the only time when I was really scared.
One day, when the friend who was sharing with me the room went away, maybe half past 6:00.
In 10 minutes, he came back full of-- he was running back.
And I was in bed, half awake.
I pretended I'm still asleep, but I wasn't.
He shook me.
He said, Janko.
Janko means Johnny in Slovak.
Get up, get up.
I said to myself, goodness me.
Now I am caught.
He said, get up quickly.
The whole area is encircled by SS and by local guards.
They are looking for Jews and for Partisans.
Because Partisan movement was already very-- we had the underground army in forming.
They made a Partisan uprising.
That happened in '44-- March, June, July.
There was a big uprising in Slovakia.
And unfortunately, the Allies didn't take advantage of it.
Anyhow, that was at that time.
And then he came back to warn me.
Nuts. ?] And he, he didn't know that I am a Jew.
If he would have known, maybe he wouldn't have saved me at all.
He just guessed because the police, German and Slovak police, they suspected always high school students or uni students, that they are the underground.
And there were a lot of Partisan and a lot of underground activity going in Bratislava, which was the capital town.
So they suspected that these people are living in the outer suburbs area, which was right.
And he, maybe, suspected that I am somehow involved in underground work.
You know?
That was good.
That was the instinct of this simple fellow.
So when he, when he woke me up, he said, quickly, Janko, dress up and I take you out.
I said, what do you mean, take me out?
He said, because the whole street is cordoned off.
Is a-- is a chain of soldiers and police and dogs.
He said, but I remember which fellow let me in.
Because when he wanted to go home, he had to show his identity card.
And the fellow who was reading it, yes, he let him in because it was encircled.
And he had so much natural instincts.
And he said, OK, I bring you out.
Because maybe when I would have walked out without him and went to any of soldiers, they would have-- and if you look very closely on falsified paper, they ask all the question.
And he said, all right.
So I had to show my identity.
He said, OK.
OK.
You can go.
And he took me out from the danger zone.
What would you do?
I didn't know what to do.
Because he had to run.
He left me there a couple of hundred yards away.
He said I-- and he was running back to work.
And I was left in the street.
No suitcase, nothing.
I had, I think, two books I took just pro forma.
So when it came to the night, I really was in trouble.
I didn't know where to go and how will I save myself.
But I remembered that one of our sales girls mentioned many times that she lives-- in the outskirts of every city, even here, there are housing commission buildings, you know?
Huge.
They were six story high, which was huge.
And they were living people there, mainly who were employed by government and, or, or people of poorer circumstances.
And they got cheaper flats there.
And I remember one of the girls told, I live in the second block of the-- they, they had a special name, this home unit block.
So I knew, and that was in that area.
So I knew I am going to look for her.
But, but that was not in the evening.
That was only in the afternoon.
And I knew she's working and she's not at home.
So how will I find her?
I didn't know which flat, what number.
But I remembered that she said on the ground floor that I live.
So I rushed in the second building on the ground floor and knocked on the first door which I had came to.
Outside, I already heard bellowing of the dogs and noises because they came and, and, and circled the whole area and were searching house to house.
You know?
So I banged on the door.
Nothing.
I said, now am I caught.
So out of desperation, I kicked the door.
And suddenly-- I was sure I, this is it.
Suddenly, the, the little window opened, because they had such a little view in the door.
And I, and I banged.
I said, please, open the door.
I-- and I think I mentioned, maybe, the name of the wife.
I don't know.
And he opened the door.
There was a 6 foot one or two inches tall man with green military trousers, green shirt, all half asleep.
And I said, please, let me in.
I am Jewish.
How did I say such a secret which is unbelievable?
And maybe that is instinct.
And this fellow looked at me.
And I said, I, are you so and so?
I know your wife.
And he said, and who are you?
I said, I am Jewish.
Please, let me in.
The whole area is encircled.
They will come any minute to your door.
They are looking for Partisans and Jews.
And he was looking.
He took me and pushed me inside and banged the door.
And there was hanging his other half of his uniform.
His jacket, which is a military jacket.
He put this one quickly on.
And really, in a few minutes, they were banging on his door.
Yeah.
He, he said, go inside in the bedroom.
He had-- that was one bedroom flat.
Kitchen and one bedroom.
Hide under the bed.
He didn't tell me twice.
I was there.
When, when he, when they banged on the door again and wanted to-- because they were searching flat to flat.
And he purposely stood behind the door and let them bang the door more and more.
And I was shivering there.
He did it very cleverly because he was employed.
He was not a military man.
He was the border guard.
And the border guards have the same, almost the same uniform-- green uniforms-- like our army.
And he had a night shift.
That, I found out after.
He had a night shift.
And when he came home early in the morning hours, he went to bed and sleep.
Because he had a night shift next day.
So he was fully awakened by me first time, and then the second time.
So he made out of-- use out of it.
He opened the door, had his jacket on.
He said, and he started to swear because he-- he said, what do want from me?
I am-- you know, anyone who had the uniform in such a regime was important.
Were either a policeman or a military man.
So they were the trusted ones.
So he made a big story.
Why?
Leave me alone.
I just came home.
I am fully asleep.
You bang on my door.
What do you want?
And they told him, we are looking for Partisans and Jews.
He said, me?
In my flat, you must be-- and he-- and he said, go to hell or something.
And he closed the door and they left him   in peace.
I was there.
We were-- I was quiet like a mouse.
And then after one or two hours, he didn't know what to do with me.
He really was a genuine, good man.
So when, when it was safe-- then later, in the afternoon, he said, my wife will come home any minute.
What shall we do with you?
I can't keep you here.
Look, I am in a position where I am a trusted man.
I am the-- he was in charge of a section of the border between Austria and Slovakia.
He said, I can't have you here.
But let's see what might my wife will say.
Of course, every good husband does it.
So she came home.
She was very surprised, of course.
She recognized me.
And she said, we have to help him.
She was looking on, on the husband and said, we have to help him.
Look at him.
How can, where will you sleep?
I said, I don't know.
I will go.
I know I bring you in danger.
I can't stay here.
And that was on the outskirts where, from the house where they were living.
A hundred yards was nothing, like grassland, you know?
A very primitive area.
And it was in, in-- what month was that?
That could have been in August or October.
Very bitter cold, you know?
So they said, what will you do?
And they went in the kitchen to discuss it, what they will do this with this little nobody.
And they came back.
And they said, you know what?
He said, Janko, we, you, you can't go out.
Where will you sleep?
So sleep here tonight.
But tomorrow morning, you have to go.
You can't bring us to such danger, especially me.
I have got a very responsible job.
I said, yes, I know.
Please, I go away tomorrow morning.
I was there about two and a half months.
Therefore, I have to shorten it.
He was such a good man and the wife.
Then they, they kept me in their one-bedroom flat.
I was sleeping in the night on the kitchen floor because they had the bedroom.
I had to give him my, my utmost assurance and hand-- we shook hands on my, my life, saying, I will be so quiet, like a mouse, during the day.
I will not use the toilet.
Because the walls were paper thin.
And all the neighbors, they knew that both are working and nobody is in the flat.
If they would have found out that I am in the flat, they would have called the police, or maybe burglar is there.
So you can imagine I was really quiet.
That was in '44. '44.
That was the other episode of my life, which-- in 1943-- from '43 to '44, they were nonstop deporting Jews.
And we were living in and out, in and out.
From '42 and '43, I came home when it was convenient.
I was living illegally.
We, we were taking such risks which we didn't realize.
And one day in 1943, it could have been in March or April, I had a naive idea, which I didn't even say to my parents.
I, I wrote a letter to the Ministry of Education, [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, I am a graduate of textile academy, which was very well known in peaceful time, and I can call it peaceful time, in Brno.
And in Slovakia, they had a small textile industry.
One of the big factories-- the biggest actually-- was in the town where I was in the military hospital.
That is called [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a big factory, 1,500, maybe 2,000 people.
And that is, for Slovakian conditions, big.

And I wrote to the ministry of education saying, because Slovakia has not a textile academy, which would be very useful for the industry which is now-- because during the war, anything which was produced in manufacturing way was booming.
They had shortage of textiles.
Anything they could produce-- ordinary cotton for bed sheets or something-- not complicated products, they couldn't make enough.
So I, I explained that, in the letter, that I would establish, with a friend of mine-- who was also a graduate of, of textile.
He went to the same class with me.
Also, a Jewish fellow.
We would establish a small textile school in, in the, under the auspices of the textile mill in Ruzomberok, which was one of the leading one.
Because they employed so many young people, which are like apprentices.
And we could start the school with the apprenticeship.
I said, they would be trained to do a better work.
And possibly later on, that can develop in a textile school, which happened.
And the textile school is existing in Slovakia, even now.
This was such fantasy, which I, when I think about it today, I can't believe it.
But I had the chutzpah to, to write-- my older brother, of course, helped me, what, to put it together.
And I send the letter to the Ministry of Education in '43.
Can you imagine what it means?
And there was a fascistic government.
And I didn't get any reply.
Two weeks, three weeks, nothing.
Then finally, I got a letter with the headline of Ministry of Education.

I opened the letter and there was only two lines written, signed by Minister of Education. 'We received your letter and we would like to have a personal interview with you.' You know, I, I thought, I, I can't believe my eyes when it was happening.
And I went to the interview.
And to my luck-- everything in life depends, very many times, on your luck, how lucky you are or how game you are.
That Minister of Education was my professor from my gymnasium years, which were not many.
He was the history professor.
A very good man.
A very intelligent fellow.
He was actually a survivor from the First World War.
He was Russian by background.
He was, I think, taken prisoner in the First World War.
And after the war was over, he settled in Bratislava, married, and became intellectual and was a professor for many years.
And he taught me history.
Very good.
And I became good pupil in history.
Matter of fact, when I went to the interview, I was only hoping that nobody should be in the room.
Because usually, they have a secretary.
Luckily, nobody was there.
So I said to him, can I close the door?
And I closed the door.
I said, Minister.
Do you remember me?
My name is so-and-so.
I am Jewish.
He said, of course I know.
And I think I remember you.
I said, you were my professor.
And I was trying my best.
He said, yes.
You were not so, such a bad student, after all.
And I think that helped me.
He said, look.
Your proposition is very interesting.
And I was operating, also, with the argument, saying, look.
If it is useful, there's no harm done.
After all, I am Jewish.
If you can help me, you will save a human life.
And he, he knew that.
He said, don't you worry.
I know that.
I know that.
He said, leave it with me.
That is not an easy decision.
Because after all, a minister can't just save a Jew.
And he saved me.
And he, he assured me in the next week or two, you will get my answer.
He didn't say yes or no.
In two weeks, I got a letter officially, again, from Ministry.
Yes, your proposition was accepted.
You have to go.
I don't know, in two or three weeks, you will travel to Ruzomberok, which was far away.
And we'll-- they, they had a better idea.
They started-- they adopted the ideas and we can possibly arrange of, of a beginning of a very simple textile school and in which the help of the factory.
They would finance it.
So he, he liked that idea.
And the Ministry will not spend thousands of dollars, you know?
So he, and he gave us, to me and to my friends, who, who did very little for it.
Matter of fact, this friend, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I, I don't think he survived.
He hardly spoke Slovak.
Because in our country, the Jewish people, which I mentioned, either were German-minded or German-educated.
They spoke more German than Slovak.
Hungarian-- and he was from the Hungarian border.
He spoke Slovak, but very badly, like my English here.
So he, he was saved by that fantasy idea.
And we both got a white degree-- decree-- which was a special paper because Ministry of Education never issued any paper like that.
And that was our survivor.
Because the paper had the heading, Ministry of Education, names.
And it was written on my own name, [?
John ?] Hirschmann.
[NON-ENGLISH] [?
John ?] Hirschmann.
But I was happy I got a piece of paper.
And we traveled, both, to Ruzomberok.
We, we got in touch with the director of the factory.
There was a factory school for the apprentices.
And we started, you can imagine, with our primitive way, I, I was trying to get-- because when you have a textile school, the beginning, you need machines.
Because you learn from books, which were very few.
And you learn through practical work.
You have to learn how to weave, this sort of weaving, how to-- like this, or the [? shirting. ?]
So we, we had to have as many machines as possible to create a work room, like a small factory.
And the students learned the, the technology from paper, and then they go the half day in the work room and see how it is done.
It is much easier to learn.
So my, my first activity I can remember, I persuaded the headmaster, who I didn't know before, but we came very friendly.
He said, look.
I speak, I spoke already a better German.
And most of the little factories in the vicinity-- because usually, industrial area is dotted with other small victories.
And they were mainly in German hands because we had a lot of ethnic Germans.
And the director of the school couldn't speak only Slovak.
He said, all right, Horak, or Hirschmann.
Horak was not my name then.
He said, I give you a letter and you will go to Kezmarok.
Kezmarok was the nearest town and it was completely a German-- like a, like a island.
Because we had a lot of villages which were completely German.
They came there in the, it was the Huguenots.
They were persecuted as Protestants in the 18th and beginning of 19th century.
And they settled in Slovakia in many times-- many towns.
And they established the textile industry or glass industry or specialized, small industries in various places.
So I went with such a cheek, with the letter of the school, of course.
They didn't write-- and I didn't-- I was young.
I didn't look Jewish, not so much.
And I went to these German factories and begged them, please give us some old machines because we need it.
On the first trip, I got four machines.
The machines were worth 5,000, 10,000 crowns, you know?
A weaving stool, it's quite a big machine.
And so I collected so many machines and in the end, the schoolyard was full.
But they came in pieces.
And so our first work was, me and my friend, to bring the machine, to put them, to assemble them together.
And that's how it started.
But we were there up to the time when there was an uprising that I mentioned before.
In middle of '44, a whole area, which was not very far from this Ruzomberok, where was this school, that was also in a mountainous area.
Three divisions of Slovakian army, which is about 60,000 to 90,000 soldiers.
So for that area, quite a big number of soldiers.
Three divisions.
They started to be-- they started the militaristic uprising against the mighty German army.
Because it was towards the end of the war.
Every intelligent German and intelligent Slovak knew that Germany is losing the war.
Then, when they went from-- no, the big town, which the Russians took over-- Stalingrad and all the other towns, you know?
And the German army was retreating.
And so the courage of the Slovakian-- because in the Slovakian army, the officers were not fascistically-- not all, but maybe half were not fascistically-minded.
Because the Slovakian country, as small as it was, about one third of the population was Protestant and two thirds were Catholic.
The Catholics were the more followers of the fascist Slovakian movement because the first president of Slovakia-- I forgot to mention-- was a Catholic priest.
He was hanged after the war as a war criminal.
We take a break.
